description: The #1 reason startups fail. Building something nobody wants.
examples: Juicero - $400 juicer nobody needed
Google Wave - solution without a clear problem
Quibi - short-form premium video nobody asked for
advice: Validate demand BEFORE building. Talk to 50+ potential users. Get pre-orders or letters of intent.

description: Poor financial planning and inability to raise follow-on funding.
examples: Jawbone - burned through $1B+ before shutting down
Essential Phone - couldn't sustain hardware costs
advice: Plan for 18-24 months of runway. Know your unit economics from day one. Cut costs early.

description: Co-founder conflicts, skill gaps, or lack of domain expertise.
signals: solo non-technical founder building tech product
examples: Many YC rejections cite team weakness
Theranos - leadership without medical device expertise
advice: Build a complementary team. Get domain experts. Have honest co-founder agreements.

description: Existing players or well-funded competitors dominate the market.
examples: Friendster lost to Facebook/MySpace
Many food delivery startups lost to DoorDash/UberEats
advice: Find your unique angle. Build a moat (network effects, data, brand). Don't compete head-on with giants.

description: Unable to find a sustainable pricing model or unit economics don't work.
examples: MoviePass - unsustainable $10/month unlimited movies
Many SaaS startups with too-low pricing
advice: Test pricing early. Understand your CAC and LTV. Charge what your product is worth.

description: Product is confusing, buggy, or doesn't solve the problem well enough.
examples: Google+ - confusing circles concept
Windows Phone - poor app ecosystem
advice: Ship MVP fast. Iterate based on feedback. Focus on core value proposition.

description: No clear path to making money despite having users.
signals: free forever with no premium tier
examples: Vine - huge user base, no revenue model
Many social apps that couldn't monetize
advice: Define your business model before launch. Test willingness to pay. Have multiple revenue streams.

description: Great product but inability to reach target customers.
examples: Many B2B SaaS tools with great tech but no distribution strategy
advice: Know your customer acquisition channels. Start marketing before launch. Build in public.

description: Not listening to user feedback or building what founders want vs what users need.
examples: Digg v4 - redesign that ignored user preferences
Snapchat redesign backlash
advice: Build feedback loops. Do weekly user interviews. Be data-driven in decisions.

description: Launching too early (market not ready) or too late (market saturated).
examples: Webvan - online grocery too early (1999)
Many crypto projects launched at market peaks
advice: Study market readiness. Look at enabling technologies and trends. Time your entry carefully.

description: Trying to do too many things at once instead of nailing one thing.
examples: Color Labs - $41M raised, unfocused product
Many startups that pivot too often
advice: Do one thing exceptionally well. Define your ICP (Ideal Customer Profile). Say no to feature requests.

description: Legal challenges, regulatory hurdles, or compliance requirements that weren't anticipated.
examples: Aereo - streaming service killed by Supreme Court
Many fintech startups hit by banking regulations
advice: Consult lawyers early. Understand regulatory landscape. Build compliance into your product.

description: Stubbornly sticking with a failing strategy instead of adapting.
examples: Blockbuster refused to adapt to streaming
Kodak ignored digital photography
advice: Set clear metrics for success. Be willing to pivot early. Run experiments constantly.

description: Growing faster than the business can sustain, hiring too aggressively.
examples: WeWork - scaled culture of excess
Fab.com - grew from 700 to 0 employees
advice: Don't scale until you have product-market fit. Grow sustainably. Keep burn rate low.

description: Founders losing motivation, health issues, or personal conflicts.
examples: Many solo founders who simply burned out after 2-3 years
advice: Take care of your health. Build a support system. Have co-founders to share the load.
